---
title: "Structuring Results for Analysis"
---


## Plotting in R {#plots_in_R}


In this final section, we build on the tabular structure introduced in the last section to produce visualizations in LISSY using R. We highlight a few particularities that users should be aware of when working in the LISSY environment, things that wouldn't arise when coding locally. Our focus is exclusively on trends (line charts), organized around six templates that users can adapt to their own countries and variables of interest.

Working from templates matters more here than it would in a local session. LISSY operates on a job-submission system rather than an interactive one: there is no way to inspect intermediate output or tweak a chart on the fly, and every change requires a new submission. Starting from a tested example that already matches your use case significantly reduces the number of iterations needed.

Each template addresses a different combination of practical dimensions: the number of countries, the type of variable being plotted (a flow variable or a categorical one), the level of disaggregation, and the number of indicators shown simultaneously. Together they cover most situations a user is likely to encounter.

The six examples are organized as follows:


- **Examples 1–2** — single variable, one country or multiple countries (flow variable; categorical variable)
- **Examples 3–4** — categorical variable across multiple countries, with increasing disaggregation (standardized variable; country-specific variable)
- **Example 5** — standardized disaggregation by a person-level variable, multiple countries side by side
- **Example 6** — multiple derived indicators in a single plot, single country


::: {.callout-important}
Unlike a local R session where plots render to screen, LISSY has no display — all output must be written to file. The `png()` function must be called before each plot. Without this, no chart is returned. When producing multiple figures in a single job, each `png()` call must use a **unique filename** — reusing a name will silently overwrite the earlier file and only the last chart will be recoverable. This is covered in detail in the [`png()` section](#png_function) at the end.
:::


### Example 1: Flow Variable Trend, Multiple Countries {#r_ex1}

This first template computes median equivalised disposable household income (`dhi`) over time for several countries simultaneously, expressed in PPP-adjusted 2021 US dollars. It illustrates the most common pattern: a flow variable aggregated to a single statistic per country-year, plotted as connected lines.

The `lissyrtools` pipeline handles the main transformations sequentially: IQR top and bottom coding to address extreme values, square-root equivalisation, PPP adjustment, and finally weighted percentile estimation. The result is passed directly to `ggplot2` via `lissyrtools::structure_to_plot()` the functions resposible to restructure our data into a tidy data frame. 


```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)
library(labelled)


data_for_plot = lissyuse(
    data = c('fr', 'us', 'uk', 'lu', 'se'),
    'dhi',
    from = 2000,
    to = 2024,
    lws = FALSE
) %>%
    map(
        ~ .x %>% filter(!is.na(dhi)) %>% mutate(new_wgt = hwgt * nhhmem)
    ) %>%
    apply_iqr_top_bottom_coding('dhi', 'hwgt') %>%
    apply_sqrt_equivalisation('dhi') %>%
    apply_ppp_adjustment(
        'dhi',
        database = 'lis',
        transformation = 'lisppp',
        base_year_ppp = 2021
    ) %>%
    run_weighted_percentiles('dhi', 'new_wgt', probs = 0.5) %>%
    structure_to_plot()


 # Plot 

png(file = paste0(USR_PDF, "/ex.1.png"), width = 1280, height = 720, res = 150)

ggplot(data_for_plot, aes(x = year, y = value, group = cname, color = cname)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    labs(
        x = "",
        y = "",
        title = "Median Disposable Household Income in US int.$ 2021",
        caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study"
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
    scale_y_continuous(
        labels = comma,
        limits = c(0, NA),
        breaks = seq(0, 55000, by = 5000)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
        panel.grid.major.y = element_line(linewidth = 0.3, color = "grey85"),
        panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
    )
```


![](/images/r_viz_1.png){width=100%}


### Example 2: Categorical Variable Trend, Single Country {#r_ex2}

This template shifts from a flow variable to a categorical one. It plots the share of employment by industry sector (`indb1`) in Germany over time, using relative frequencies rather than a monetary aggregate. The data preparation is straightforward: `lissyrtools::run_weighted_count()` with `percent = TRUE` computes within-wave shares directly, and `lissyrtools::structure_to_plot()` reshapes the result for plotting.

This is a useful template for any LIS variable that takes a small number of discrete values and for which tracking compositional change over time is of interest (employment type, household structure, education level, and so on).

::: {.callout-note}
**Variable availability:** Industry of employment is not available for all country-waves in LIS. Before running this template on a new country, check coverage in the LIS variable documentation, or using the methods described in the earlier chapters of this guide.
:::


```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

data_for_plot = lissyuse(
    data = c('de'),
    'indb1',
    from = 2000,
    to = 2024,
    lws = FALSE
) %>%
    map(~ .x %>% filter(!is.na(indb1)))


png(
    file = paste0(USR_PDF, "/ex.2.png"),
    width = 1280,
    height = 720,
    res = 150
)

run_weighted_count(
    data_for_plot,
    var_name = "indb1",
    wgt_name = "ppopwgt",
    percent = TRUE
) %>%
    structure_to_plot() %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = year, y = value, group = category, color = category)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    labs(
        x = "",
        y = "",
        title = "Employment by Industry Sector in Germany (2000–2024)",
        caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study"
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(
        palette = "Paired",
        labels = \(x) str_wrap(x, width = 25)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
        legend.position = "right",
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
        legend.key.height = unit(1.5, "cm"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
    )
```


![](/images/r_viz_2.png){width=100%}


### Example 3: Categorical Variable Trend, Multiple Countries {#r_ex3}


This template extends Example 2 to multiple countries, using occupation group (`occa1`) rather than industry. Rather than producing one plot per country and combining them manually, `facet_wrap(~cname)` handles the multi-country layout natively within a single `ggplot2` call, producing side-by-side subplots with a shared legend.

```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

data_for_plot <- lissyuse(
    data = c('pe', 'lu'),
    'occa1',
    from = 2000,
    to = 2024,
    lws = FALSE
) %>%
    map(~ .x %>% filter(!is.na(occa1)))


png(file = paste0(USR_PDF, "/ex3.png"), width = 1280, height = 720, res = 150)

run_weighted_count(
    data_for_plot,
    var_name = "occa1",
    wgt_name = "ppopwgt",
    percent = TRUE
) %>%
    structure_to_plot() %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = year, y = value, group = category, color = category)) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    facet_wrap(~cname, ncol = 2) +
    labs(
        x = "",
        y = "",
        title = "Employment by Occupation Group (3 Categories)",
        caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study"
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(
        palette = "Paired",
        labels = \(x) str_wrap(x, width = 20)
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
        breaks = scales::breaks_width(10)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
        legend.position = "bottom",
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.minor.x = element_blank(),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.5),
        panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "grey85", linewidth = 0.3),
        panel.spacing = unit(1.5, "lines")
    )
```

![](/images/r_viz_3.png){width=100%}


### Example 4: Country-Specific Disaggregation, Multiple Countries {#stata_ex4}

The previous examples all used standardized LIS variables. This template instead uses `region_c`, a country-specific variable that is not harmonized across countries. The region filtering is applied after `lissyrtools::structure_to_plot()` using `str_detect()` on the category labels, selecting a meaningful subset of regions per country to keep the chart readable. The specific regions chosen here are illustrative.



```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)


data_for_plot <- lissyuse(
    data = c('de', 'it'),
    c('dhi', 'region_c'),
    from = 2000,
    to = 2024,
    lws = FALSE
) %>%
    map(~ .x %>% filter(!is.na(dhi)) %>% mutate(new_wgt = nhhmem * hwgt)) %>%
    apply_iqr_top_bottom_coding('dhi', 'hwgt') %>%
    apply_sqrt_equivalisation('dhi') %>%
    apply_ppp_adjustment('dhi', database = 'lis', transformation = 'lisppp') %>%
    run_weighted_mean('dhi', 'new_wgt', by = 'region_c') %>%
    structure_to_plot() %>%
    filter(
        str_detect(
            category,
            paste(
                c(
                    'Lazio',
                    'Lombardia',
                    'Campania',
                    'Sicilia',
                    'Bavaria',
                    'Westphalia',
                    'Baden-Wuerttemberg',
                    'Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania'
                ),
                collapse = "|"
            )
        )
    )

png(file = paste0(USR_PDF, "/ex4.png"), width = 1280, height = 720, res = 150)

ggplot(
    data_for_plot,
    aes(x = year, y = value, group = category, color = category)
) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    facet_wrap(~cname, ncol = 2) +
    labs(
        x = "",
        y = "",
        title = "Mean Equivalised Disposable Income by Region",
        subtitle = "Germany and Italy, selected regions (CPI-adjusted, PPP)",
        caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study"
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(
        palette = "Paired",
        labels = \(x) str_wrap(x, width = 20)
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
        labels = comma,
        ,
        limits = c(0, NA),
        breaks = seq(0, 50000, by = 5000)
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
        legend.position = "right",
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 8),
        legend.key.height = unit(1.5, "cm"),
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40"),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10)
    )
```

![](/images/r_viz_4.png){width=100%}


### Example 5: Standardized Disaggregation, Multiple Countries {#stata_ex5}


This template uses `sex` to disaggregate median labour income (`pi11`) for working-age employed individuals in Sweden and Germany. The sample is restricted to respondents aged 25–55 who are employed, a standard restriction for labour income analysis. `facet_wrap("cname")` handles the multi-country layout with a shared legend, and PPP adjustment is applied to express wages in comparable units across countries.


```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

lis_data <- lissyuse(
    data = c("se", "de"),
    vars = c("age", "sex", "pi11", "emp"),
    from = 2002
)


png(file = paste0(USR_PDF, "ex5.png"), width = 1280, height = 720, res = 150)

lis_data %>%
    map(
        ~ .x %>%
            filter(age > 24 & age < 56) %>%
            filter(emp == 1),
        filter(!is.na(pi11))
    ) %>%
    apply_ppp_adjustment(
        "pi11",
        database = "lis",
        transformation = "lisppp"
    ) %>%
    run_weighted_mean("pi11", "ppopwgt", by = "sex") %>%
    structure_to_plot() %>%
    ggplot(
        aes(x = year, y = value, group = category, color = category)
    ) +
    geom_line() +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    labs(
        x = "",
        y = "",
        title = "Working-Age Average Wage",
        caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study",
        color = "Gender"
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(
        labels = comma,
        limits = c(0, NA),
        breaks = seq(0, 60000, by = 5000)
    ) +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "bottom",
        plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10)
    ) +
    facet_wrap("cname")
```


![](/images/r_viz_5.png){width=100%}


### Example 6: Multiple Indicators, Multiple Countries {#stata_ex6}


The final template plots multiple derived indicators on the same axes and extends this to more than one country by producing one chart per country within the same job. Using the United Kingdom and Germany as examples, it computes three percentile ratios (90/10, 90/50, 50/10) of equivalised disposable income and plots them as a time series for each country in separate images.

The three ratios are computed separately using `lissyrtools::run_weighted_ratios()` and bound into a single data frame with a country and indicator identifier. An auxiliary function `get_country_graph()` then handles both the plot construction and the `png()` call for each country, keeping the final loop concise.

::: {.callout-important}
**Dynamic export naming:** Because this template produces one chart per country within the same job submission, each `png()` call must use a unique filename. Here this is achieved inside `get_country_graph()` by embedding the country name directly in the path: `paste0(USR_PDF, "/ratios_", country, ".png")`. Without this, each iteration would overwrite the previous file and only the last country's chart would be recoverable from LISSY output.
:::



```{r, eval = FALSE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE}
library(lissyrtools)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggpubr)
library(scales)


lis_datasets <- lissyuse(
    data = c("uk", "de"),
    vars = c("dhi"),
    from = 1970
)


ready_data <- lis_datasets %>%
    map(~ .x %>% filter(!is.na(dhi))) %>%
    apply_sqrt_equivalisation("dhi")


ratios <- bind_rows(
    # Ratio 90/10
    run_weighted_ratios(
        ready_data,
        var_name = "dhi",
        wgt_name = "hwgt",
        upper_percentile = 0.9,
        lower_percentile = 0.1
    ) %>%
        structure_to_plot() %>%
        mutate(indicator = "p_90_10"),
    # Ratio 90/50
    run_weighted_ratios(
        ready_data,
        var_name = "dhi",
        wgt_name = "hwgt",
        upper_percentile = 0.9,
        lower_percentile = 0.5
    ) %>%
        structure_to_plot() %>%
        mutate(indicator = "p_90_50"),
    # Ratio 50/10
    run_weighted_ratios(
        ready_data,
        var_name = "dhi",
        wgt_name = "hwgt",
        upper_percentile = 0.5,
        lower_percentile = 0.1
    ) %>%
        structure_to_plot() %>%
        mutate(indicator = "p_50_10")
)

# Auxilliary Printing Function
get_country_graph <- function(country) {
    p <- ggplot(
        ratios %>% filter(cname == country),
        aes(x = year, y = value, group = indicator, color = indicator)
    ) +
        geom_line() +
        geom_point(size = 2.5) +
        labs(
            x = "",
            y = "Ratio",
            title = paste0(country, " — Inequality Ratios"),
            caption = "Source: Luxembourg Income Study"
        ) +
        scale_y_continuous(
            labels = comma,
            limits = c(0, 5.5),
            breaks = seq(0, 6, by = 1)
        ) +
        scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set1") +
        theme_minimal() +
        theme(
            legend.position = "right",
            legend.title = element_blank(),
            plot.caption = element_text(hjust = 0, size = 8, color = "grey50"),
            plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12)
        )

    fileName <- paste0(USR_PDF, "/ratios_", country, ".png")

    print(png(file = fileName, width = 1280, height = 720, res = 150))
    print(p)
}


# Final Loop per Country
countries <- unique(ratios$cname)
for (country in countries) {
    get_country_graph(country)
}
```


![](/images/r_viz_6.png){width=100%}





### The `png()` Function in LISSY {#png_function}

In a standard R session, plots render directly to screen. In LISSY, there is no display — all output must be written to file. The `png()` function serves this purpose, and its correct use is essential for any visualization job.

It controls two things:

- **Output filename:** The `file` argument defines where the chart is saved. Always write to `USR_PDF`, the LISSY-provided path for user output files.
- **Plot dimensions and resolution:** The `width`, `height`, and `res` arguments control the pixel dimensions and dots-per-inch of the exported image. The values used throughout these examples (`width = 1280`, `height = 720`, `res = 150`) produce a 1280×720px image at 150 DPI, which renders clearly in most contexts.


::: {.callout-important}
**Unique filenames are mandatory when producing multiple plots in one job.** Each `png()` call opens a new graphics device writing to the specified path. If two calls share the same filename, the second will overwrite the first, and only the last chart will be recoverable from LISSY output. Use descriptive, numbered, or otherwise distinct names — as done throughout these examples — whenever a single job produces more than one figure.
:::
